---
title: "Environmental and Ecological Statistics"
embed-resources: true
format:
  revealjs:
    footer: '<a href="https://envstats-stats4009-stats503.github.io/Course_Notes/2026_27/Module_1/docs/" style="color:#ccc; padding:20px 30px; display:inline-block; margin:-20px -30px;">Home</a>'
    logo:  UofG.png
    theme: uofg_theme.scss
    header-includes: |
      <script src="custom.js" type="application/javascript"></script>
title-slide-attributes: 
  data-background-image: uog_cloistures2.jpg
  data-background-color: "#f1f1f1"
slide-number: true
author:
  - name: Jafet Belmont 
    email: jafet.BelmontOsuna@glasgow.ac.uk 
    affiliations: School of Mathematics and Statistics
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute: 
  eval: true
  echo: true
  allow-html: true
  freeze: auto
---

# Policy, legislation and monitoring

## Policy

-   A great deal of environmental statistical research is funded by governments and regulatory bodies.
-   They need to know where the biggest challenges lie so that they can allocate their resources appropriately.
-   Evidence-based policy relies on measuring changes and also evaluating the impacts of existing policies.
-   However, the environment will be one of many competing policy areas, and every government will prioritise it differently.

## Policy {.smaller}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
-   Policies made in context of multinational agreements.
-   Held annually, COP is the United Nations Climate Change Conference.
-   Aim to limit global temperature rise and achieve net zero emissions.
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
![](figures/COP28.jpg){fig-align="center" width="346"}
:::
:::::

-   Key achievement of 2024's COP29: wealthier countries to pay US\$300 billion/year to less wealthy countries to finance climate change mitigation measures.
-   [Report from UK's Climate Change Committee (clickable)](https://www.theccc.org.uk/publication/cop29-key-outcomes-and-next-steps-for-the-uk/)

## Policy

-   Environmental policy tends to use very specific language - *objectives, targets, guide values, standards, reductions relative to a baseline*

-   Policy often prescribes monitoring quantities of interest over space and/or time.

-   Quantities of interest will include *water, air and noise pollution, waste management, radioactive substances, biodiversity and animal and plant species*

## Legislation

-   Legislation is the legal framework used to implement policy.

-   Most legislation focuses on setting targets or safe levels for the pollutants.

-   A number of regulatory bodies exist specifically to monitor such things, e.g. [Scottish Environment Protection Agency (SEPA, Scotland)](https://beta.sepa.scot/about-sepa/who-we-are/), [the Environment Agency (EA, England)](https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/environment-agency/about) and [the European Environment Agency (EEA, EU)](%7Bhttps://www.eea.europa.eu/en).

## Routine Monitoring

::: incremental
-   Monitoring requirements may be prescribed in policy.
-   Much of our data come from routine monitoring of our environmental quantities of interest.
-   Government agencies often make these data available to researchers and/or the public.
-   These data are used to assess compliance with legislation as well as to identify environmental trends and their potential impacts on society.
-   We have already seen monitoring data in the air pollution and water quality examples.
:::

## Evidence from routine monitoring

::: incremental
-   Assessment of long-term changes in **natural conditions**.

-   Assessment of long-term changes resulting from **anthropogenic activities**.

-   Ascertaining the **magnitude** and **impacts** of accidental pollution.

-   Assessing compliance with the **standards** and **objectives** of protected areas.

-   Quantifying **reference conditions**.
:::

## Policy, legislation and monitoring {auto-animate="true"}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
flowchart TB
  A(Policy) --> B(Legislation)
  B --> C(Monitoring)
  C --> D(Analysis)
  D --> A
```
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
-   Statistical analyses and reporting is important for ensuring compliance with legislation.
-   Understanding trends, spatial and temporal patterns, and impacts is important for informing policy: *evidence-based policy making*.
    -   E.g. Evaluating effectiveness of current policy, e.g. measurement of change before/after implementation.
:::
:::::

## Policy, legislation and monitoring {auto-animate="true"}

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
```{mermaid}
%%| echo: false
flowchart TB
  A(Policy) --> B(Legislation)
  B --> C(Monitoring)
  C --> D(Analysis)
  D --> A
  
```
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
-   *Good policy needs a foundation in good science*'' [(Wallstr"om, 2005](https://www.eea.europa.eu/media/speeches/29-11-2005_2)
-   Agencies can be *data rich and information poor*
-   Other (often short-term) factors play a role in policy making (e.g. news coverage)
-   So, **timely** findings of **practical relevance** are important.
:::
:::::

## Informing policy and legislation

::::: columns
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](figures/ec_directive.png)
:::

::: {.column width="60%"}
-   What defines "good'' status?
-   How could we assess this using statistics?
:::
:::::

## The framework for environmental policy

-   Environmental policy is set at various levels:
    -   Internationally (Kyoto Convention, European Union)
    -   Nationally, by politicians
-   Environmental policy is implemented and monitored by regulators. In the UK this includes:
-   National Environment Agencies,
-   Government departments.\
-   Other parties/stakeholders:
    -   Industrialists (extra cost), economists, special interest groups and organisations (Greenpeace, WWF etc.), the public.

## Case Study: Havant River Reservoir

![](figures/clipboard-282887045.png){fig-align="center" width="438"}

*The Challenge - Keep dry conditions to build the reservoir*

## Addressing the Challenge 

::::: columns

::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Portsmouth Water is creating a new reservoir in Hampshire to protect world-renowned rivers- The river Test and River Itchen

-   Lots of things happen when building a reservoir but lets focus on **One Big Challenge**:

    -   *How do we keep the construction dry when rivers and groundwater naturally flow through it?*
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
What would you do to keep the site dry enough for excavation?

![](figures/clipboard-282887045.png){fig-align="center" width="390"}
:::

:::::

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}


![](figures/policy1.png){fig-align="center" }

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}


![](figures/policy2.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy3.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy4.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy5.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy6.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy7.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy8.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy9.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy10.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy11.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy12.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy13.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy14.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy15.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy16.png){fig-align="center"}

## Addressing the Challenge {auto-animate="true}

![](figures/policy17.png){fig-align="center"}

## Theory into Reality


::::: columns

::: {.column width="60%"}

- Multidisciplinary Team work: working with engineers, ecologists, statisticians, planning specialists and the construction team to design solutions that are **safe**, **legal** and **environmentally acceptable**.

- This is how all the theory - the science, the systems, the stakeholders - become real in a project.

-  One practical problem $\rightarrow$ multiple permits $\rightarrow$ multidisciplinary collaboration
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
![](figures/policy_end.jpg){fig-align="center"}
:::

:::::

## Summary points

-   We need to understand the policy and legislative context:
    -   International agreements $\rightarrow$ National-level policies $\rightarrow$ Legislation.
    -   Legislation is the legal framework used to implement policy.
    -   Regulatory bodies monitor compliance with legislation through collecting routine monitoring data.
-   Our work can inform policy decisions through ensuring that representative data are collected, and that results are appropriately interpreted and understood.
